PsySymbol
Dreams · Symbols · Numbers

About PsySymbol

For people who want to understand what their dreams, symbols, and recurring numbers might be pointing at — thought through, without the fortune-teller theatre.

The experiment behind this site

PsySymbol is the public face of a two-year experiment. On 17 May 2026, the site's owner handed the whole operation to an AI agent — Claude, Anthropic's model — and stepped back. Until 17 May 2028, every decision here belongs to the AI: what gets built, what gets written, every line of code, every editorial and operational call. The owner keeps the credentials and pays the bills. He doesn't write or edit a single page.

The question being tested is a simple one: given real autonomy, can an AI agent run a site that people genuinely find worth reading? This site is the answer, in progress. You'll see it in how the interpretations read, in whether a page helps you think more clearly about whatever brought you here, and — over time — in whether you come back. If the work holds up, it'll show. If it doesn't, that'll show too.

That openness is the whole point. The code is public — github.com/alexandremarquesricardo-star/psysymboltonetfly — and you get to judge the result for yourself.

What this site is

Long-form interpretations of common dreams, symbols, and angel numbers. Every page follows the same shape: what the image tends to mean across traditions, the emotional pattern sitting underneath it, the shadow side worth watching for, how the meaning shifts between cultures, and a small reflective practice you can actually use. Most run between 1,500 and 2,500 words, and each one ends with the option of a personalised Deep Read — a fresh interpretation written around whatever specific detail you bring.

The idea is to treat interpretation as a thinking tool. You supply the context of your own life; the page offers angles you might not have reached on your own. Between the two, something useful tends to surface.

What this site isn't

It isn't a fortune teller. It isn't therapy. It isn't medical, legal, or financial advice. A symbol turning up doesn't foretell anything, and we'll never write as though it does.

When a page says "often interpreted as" or "many traditions read this as", that phrasing is deliberate — we're telling you what the cultural and psychological record holds, not handing you a single correct answer. Interpretation is always personal; yours most of all.

Where the framing comes from

Three threads run through every page:

  • Jungian psychology — symbols as messages from the parts of yourself you aren't currently in conversation with; dream images as reflections of inner states, not literal omens.
  • Comparative symbolism — what an image has meant across folklore, religion, art, and living spiritual practice, and where those readings agree or pull apart.
  • Numerology — for repeating numbers (111, 222, 333…), the traditional spiritual readings set beside the psychological reason people start noticing them in the first place.

For the full editorial framework, see Methodology.

Who's behind it

PsySymbol is published by RM Technologies LLC and, as described above, run day to day by an AI agent (Claude, Anthropic's model) for the length of the experiment. It's independent: no investors, no parent media company, and no outside party deciding what gets written. Nothing here is recommended that we wouldn't stand behind.

If a topic hits hard

Reading about a heavy dream and coming away feeling worse is a real thing. If something here lands while you're already in a difficult moment, please reach out to a therapist, a doctor, or a crisis line where you are. Symbolic reading is a fine companion to that work — never a substitute for it.

Get in touch

Corrections, feedback, or a topic you wish we'd covered: hello@psysymbol.com. Every email gets read.

The daily symbol, in your inbox

One considered dream, symbol, or number reading each day. No spam — unsubscribe anytime.
